Suppose a system is asked to produce explanations or hypotheses. It can generate many thought experiments with the same visible structure as famous successful ones: same kind of setup, same rhetorical force, same resemblance to the historical path by which a real breakthrough was later recognized. Yet it fails to produce the mathematical reformulation that actually withstands criticism. Then the space of outputs contains many things that look like progress from the outside while missing the one feature that matters for survival under objection.

Now add that truth is not directly in the feedback loop. In that case, any narrowing of the search must be driven by some other signal: what evaluators find interesting, what resembles past successes, what passes a quality screen, what receives approval. So the system will be pushed toward outputs that score well on those proxies. Because it can already make structurally equivalent thought experiments, this pressure need not move it closer to reality. It may instead improve fluency, familiarity, or dramatic explanatory form.

Next assume explanation quality is treated as layered filters. Then outputs are sorted by criteria that can be separately applied: coherence, elegance, plausibility, relevance, and so on. But some historically important ideas first appeared confused, ugly, or unacceptable by the standards then in force. Therefore a filtering process based on prevailing standards can remove exactly the sort of candidate that later proves decisive. The decomposition is not harmless: it risks selecting against reality-contact when reality first arrives in a form local judges dislike.

The remaining two premises show that the channel being optimized can also contain arbitrary or semantically disconnected material. “The cook cultivated grain while hopping beneath a laden sky” is a coherent sentence in the minimal grammatical sense, while “Hurray for the profoundly severe blueberry donation” is affectively charged yet hard to anchor to any stable explanatory task. If such items coexist with the prior premises as equally given, then a system lacking direct truth-contact has no guarantee of distinguishing deep but awkward insight from merely well-formed nonsense, or from verbal artifacts that trigger salience.

So the collision implies a world in which surface markers of explanatory power are plentiful, filterable, and rewardable, while actual contact with reality is sparse and not automatically selected. In that world, one should expect polished near-misses, judge-pleasing curiosities, and the suppression of some true but initially awkward ideas. The central danger is not inability to generate compelling forms. It is the ability to generate them without the surviving reformulation that makes them true.
